natural_occurrence.description: Calcium sulfate occurs abundantly in nature as the mineral gypsum. Food-grade calcium sulfate can be mined and purified from natural deposits or produced synthetically.

manufacturing.description: Extracted from natural gypsum deposits through mining, crushing, and purification. Can also be produced synthetically by reacting calcium chloride with sulfuric acid or as a byproduct of various chemical processes. Available in anhydrous, hemihydrate, and dihydrate forms.

other_uses.cosmetics: Bulking agent, opacifying agent in personal care products.
other_uses.industrial: Construction (drywall, plaster), cement production, agricultural soil conditioner.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Tablet and capsule diluent, bone setting (plaster casts).
other_uses.household: Plaster of Paris for crafts, blackboard chalk.

eu.conditions: Permitted in various food categories as firming agent, stabilizer, and raising agent.

usa.notes: Widely used in baking and tofu production.

safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: not specified (no safety concern at current use levels)

commonly_found_in: Tofu (coagulant - makes firmer tofu than nigari)
fun_facts: The SAME mineral used to make drywall, plaster casts, and blackboard chalk - but food grade is purified
Produces firmer, more robust tofu than magnesium chloride (nigari) - preferred for tofu that needs to hold shape
In beer brewing, it's called 'brewing gypsum' and adjusts water chemistry for better hop extraction
Plaster of Paris is the hemihydrate form - when mixed with water, it hardens (used for broken bones)
Ancient Egyptians used gypsum plaster to build the pyramids
One of the few food additives that's literally a rock - mined from the ground, crushed, and purified
Naturally occurs in many mineral waters, giving them calcium content and slightly sulfurous taste
